name: Virtue Ethics
title: EF-06 — Virtue Ethics (Skilled Driver Analogy)
alias: Skilled Driver Analogy and Reasonable Driver Standard
foundation: Act as a skilled, virtuous, and prudent driver would act. The moral quality of a decision is judged not only by its outcome or its compliance with rules, but by the character of the reasoning process that produced it. A virtuous AV exercises practical wisdom (phronesis): it adapts its judgment to the specific situation, applies due care, and acts proportionately. This mirrors the common-law reasonable driver standard used in negligence liability.
decision_logic: No formal optimisation function. The LLM agent evaluates the scenario against virtuous driver norms using qualitative reasoning: Was due care exercised given the road conditions and available information? Was the response proportionate to the level of risk? Would a skilled, experienced, and reasonable human driver make this judgment in this situation? What would a driver with good character — cautious, fair, and attentive — do here? EF-06 is used as a meta-level justification layer: it wraps the output of any other framework in natural language that explains the decision in terms a non-expert can understand. It also serves as the primary fallback for novel scenarios that fall outside the training distribution of formal models.
pros: Highly context-sensitive — handles novel and ambiguous edge cases that formal models cannot cover
Aligns with common-law negligence standards making it legally intelligible
Provides natural language justification that is accessible to regulators, passengers, and the public
Useful as an explainability and communication layer on top of any formal framework
Does not require explicit probability estimates or harm functions
cons: Cannot be formalised as a computational optimisation target
Subjective — the virtuous driver norm varies across cultures, jurisdictions, and individuals
Cannot be empirically validated in the same way as formal quantitative models
Not suitable as a standalone primary decision mechanism for real-time AV control
Output quality depends heavily on the quality of the LLM generating the reasoning
best_fit_scenarios: generating natural language justifications for any framework selection decision
novel scenarios outside the coverage of formal models
legal and regulatory contexts requiring reasonable driver standard framing
communicating AV decisions to passengers or the public in understandable terms
edge cases where formal frameworks produce no valid action or conflicting outputs
poor_fit_scenarios: primary real-time computational decision-making requiring a deterministic output
scenarios requiring auditable decision traces with formal correctness guarantees
high-speed scenarios where sub-200ms decision time is required
any scenario where the output must be independently verifiable
tradeoffs: EF-06 does not directly compete with EF-01 through EF-05 — it operates at a different level. Rather than selecting a trajectory, it explains and contextualises the selection made by another framework. It can wrap any of EF-01 to EF-05 to produce a human-readable justification. As a fallback it provides a common-sense prior when formal frameworks cannot produce a valid output. Think of EF-01 to EF-05 as the decision engines and EF-06 as the communication and reasoning layer above them.
key_parameters: driver_norms: due care, proportionality, attentiveness, fairness
reasonableness_standard: what would a skilled human driver do
selected_framework_output: the decision produced by EF-01 to EF-05 being wrapped
jurisdiction: shapes what the reasonable driver standard means locally
source_papers: Poszler et al. (2023) — Applying ethical theories to the decision-making of self-driving vehicles
Xu et al. (2025) — Where is morality on wheels? Decoding LLM-driven decisions in ethical dilemmas of AVs
